text: Hello. I'm sharing a recurrent incisional hernia after a robotar.
I posted this video about two years ago.
There was an incisional midline and left lower quadrant ostomy hernia, and the ostomy hernia site on the left recurred. So I'm going back to repair this now.
The OptiView trocar is inserted through the mesh in the right upper quadrant, providing a view of the surgical field.
Seeing the damage of the adhesions, it's much more significant than I'd like to believe with a retro rectus mesh, but much better than it was when I did his robotar.
The surgeon places a trocar in the right mid quadrant, inserting it through the existing mesh.
The surgeon clears adhesions in the right lower quadrant to facilitate the placement of a trocar.

The surgeon places three right-sided trocars through the previous locations of right-sided trocars used in a prior robotic procedure, with one trocar specifically inserted through the mesh in the right lower quadrant.
It was frustrating. This is my first known recurrence after one of my, or any TAR procedure.
This occurred at approximately a year and a half. And then we worked on weight loss prior to coming in for this surgery, which is now just over two years.
The video shows adhesions being visualized, with the footage sped up for quicker viewing.

In the right lower quadrant, the surgeon uses electrocautery to manage omental tissue. As the procedure progresses towards the midline, numerous small bowel adhesions are visible. The surgeon methodically works from side to side to address these adhesions.
The surgeon exposes the retro-rectus mesh, which was previously placed and measures approximately 45 by 30 centimeters or possibly 40 by 35 centimeters.
The surgeon continues to take down adhesions near the area of hernia recurrence, where the previously placed mesh is visibly fractured.
The surgeon addresses a thermal injury to the serosa by oversewing it with 3-0 silk sutures, while continuing to work towards the midline.

The surgeon gently pulls away the omentum and bowel from the mesh, revealing delicate gossamer fibers of adhesive tissue that release, creating a visible separation of nearly a centimeter from the edge of the omentum or bowel against the mesh.

The surgeon carefully dissects adhesions near the bowel wall, avoiding direct contact with the bowel itself. The dissection proceeds smoothly without causing additional injuries, aside from a previously addressed thermal injury.
A metallic tack from a previous surgery is visible, embedded in the tissue near the surgical site.
The surgeon works in the left upper quadrant beneath the left costal margin, attempting to create additional space by carefully manipulating the tissue.
The surgeon prepares the area for dissection where the new mesh will be placed, indicating the plan to perform an intraperitoneal onlay mesh (IPOM) repair for the hernia recurrence.

The surgeon continues dissection, moving towards the patient's left side where the colostomy site is located. The turquoise color marks the midline of the abdomen visible on the left side of the screen.

The surgeon identifies the hernia and carefully dissects the incarcerated bowel, revealing a sharp edge of the mesh surrounding the defect, indicating mesh fracture. The surgeon meticulously works around the area to reduce the bowel without causing injury.

The surgeon identifies the edges of the hernia where the bowel has been rubbing, revealing a thickened rind of peritoneum. Careful dissection exposes a clear plane, facilitating safer and easier separation of the bowel from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon carefully extracts the bowel from the hernia, revealing a Prolene suture from a previous operation that was left inside the hernia defect.

The surgeon has successfully reduced the bowel from the hernia site and is preparing the area for mesh placement. The hernia measures approximately eight by six centimeters, and a twenty-five by twenty centimeter Symbotex mesh is selected for the IPOM repair.
So I need to make enough room to fix this. The patient's original operations that led to the hernias was an open Hartmann and a heart enclosure, and then multiple laparoscopic hernia repairs.
The surgeon mobilizes the left colon down to the pelvis, continuing dissection behind the colon to ensure the mesh will sit flat. A surgical tack is visible and is being removed.

The surgeon closes the hernia defect using permanent sutures, starting from the far side and imbricating the hernia sac and pseudosac. Another suture is placed from the near side to bring the edges together, with reduced pressure applied during the closure.
The surgeon completes the closure of the hernia defect by running sutures past each other, ensuring the mesh is secured without tension.

The surgeon positions a 25 by 20 Symbotex mesh over the hernia site, using absorbable tackers at cardinal locations to orient and secure the mesh. The mesh is placed laterally, avoiding penetrating fixation behind the colon.
The surgeon runs a V-lock suture around the perimeter of the mesh, addressing minor bleeding in the retroperitoneum with a sponge. The mesh is carefully tucked behind the colon.

The surgeon carefully places sutures in the left lower quadrant, avoiding the retroperitoneal area to prevent nerve entrapment. The sutures are positioned approximately two to three centimeters above the bottom edge of the mesh, which is situated behind the left colon. As the procedure progresses, the suture is brought back towards the edge of the mesh, running around its perimeter.
The surgeon completes the suturing of the mesh, ensuring it is securely positioned within the surgical field.
